title: Treatment-resistant varroa mites spread in Queensland and New South Wales
author: Jennifer Nichols
fullText: The world's worst honey bee parasite is genetically mutating, making it resistant to common treatments used to save hives.Authorities say treatment-resistant varroa mites were found in New South Wales in January, and have now been confirmed at three sites in south-east Queensland, including the Southern Downs and Ipswich.The varroa mites hitch rides on bees. (Supplied: Norma May Honey)The mutation makes common insecticides, known as pyrethroids, less effective, making it harder for beekeepers to protect their hives from the destructive pest.Biosecurity Queensland's varroa mite program manager, Rob Stephens, said he was awaiting further test results to confirm more cases, but expected the chemical resistance to spread.Biosecurity Queensland varroa mite program manager Rob Stephens expects increased chemical resistance. (Supplied: Rob Stephens)"It's been a hard time for our beekeepers since varroa got here in 2022," he said."This is just the next thing they have to face, unfortunately."These varroa mites on bee larvae were killed with heat treatment. (Supplied: Norma May Honey)World's worst bee parasiteVarroa destructor mites invade drone and worker brood cells — the nursery of the hive — feeding and breeding on baby European honey bees.They directly inject viruses, can spread pathogens, and leave weakened hives vulnerable to secondary attack by other pests, like small hive beetles.Tiny varroa mites feeding on drone pupae, which were cut out of a beehive. (Wolfgang Kumm/Getty Images)The pest has spread from New South Wales to Queensland, the ACT, Victoria, and South Australia.But so far, Mr Stephens said the chemical resistance has only been confirmed in NSW and Queensland."Some of the (affected) apiaries that we've tested were the result of tracing (to NSW)," he said."Others were beekeepers who had reported treatment failures."Queen bee breeder Corinne Jordan says the varroa "invasion" phase is difficult to manage. (Supplied: Corinne Jordan)'A perfect storm'Corinne Jordan breeds queen bees at Carbrook, halfway between Brisbane and the Gold Coast.She was shocked when nearly 90 per cent of varroa mites survived during three tests on one of her pyrethroid-treated beehives at Victoria Point."It's just a perfect storm, we're still in the invasion wave, and we're all scrambling just to save our bees," Ms Jordan said.Beekeepers test their hives to count mite infestations. (Supplied: Norma May Honey)So far, tests on her apiary sites have not confirmed the genetic mutation, but Ms Jordan believed her bees were under siege."I'm actually now in full crisis mode," she said."Trying to go through and pull chemical treatments out of all hives that have them in and put a more effective treatment plan in place."Trevor Weatherhead says varroa mites are killing feral honey bee hives. (ABC News: Ashleigh Stevenson)Alternate chemical treatmentsQueensland Beekeeping Association president Trevor Weatherhead said treatment plans should include rotating miticides with different active ingredients, to prevent further development of chemical resistance."The last thing we want is resistance to Amitraz, (the alternative treatments to pyrethroids)," Mr Weatherhead said.An adult female varroa destructor mite. (Supplied: Gilles San Martin)"That's what's happening in the US at the moment, causing big (honey bee) losses in the last winter."He said it was crucial that apiarists regularly monitored their hives and checked for updated information on the Australian Honey Bee Industry Council's website.Honey bees have escaped to set up home in hollow trees. (Supplied: Scott Whitaker/Hinterland Bees)Pollination impactAs the pest spreads, Mr Weatherhead said most feral honey bee colonies living in Australian bushland would die out, ending the pollination they provided to farms and gardens."A lot of people even now are saying that they're see less honey bees around for pollination of things like watermelons and zucchinis," he said.Most unmanaged honey bee hives will struggle to survive varroa mites. (ABC Rural: Jennifer Nichols)He said those dead feral colonies provided a reinfection point, as bees from treated hives robbed them of their honey, picking up new mites in the process"While they're in there sucking up a bit of honey, the mites hop onto the bees, and they come back to the hive, and you've got a re-infestation of mites."Honey bees that escape to the bush are predicted to die. (Supplied: Rob Stephens)Since the first detection in Queensland in March 2025, almost 1,000 varroa mite infestations have been reported in 21 local government areas.There are 161,377 registered beehives in Queensland, with 439 commercial beekeepers and 9,562 hobbyists.Good hive management will be crucial for honey bee survival. (ABC Rural: Jennifer Nichols)But Mr Weatherhead predicted the number of backyard beekeepers would shrink as the cost and labour of caring for bees affected by the mites increased."We estimate that about 50 per cent of amateur beekeepers won't be keeping bees within about a year or two years," he said.The varroa mite parasite captured using magnification. (Supplied: Norma May Honey)The 'invasion phase'Mr Weatherhead said during the early "invasion phase" of the spread, non-chemical means of managing varroa were difficult, especially in warmer climates where bees breed all year.Despite the stress and cost, Ms Jordan said she was determined to find a solution.Corinne Jordan is optimistic, despite the current challenges. (Supplied: Corinne Jordan)She received a Queensland government Community Bee Innovation Fund grant, which she plans to use to fund further genomic testing, monitoring of mite populations, and workshops for beekeepers."I love beekeeping and I'm committed to trying to save them and continue with the breeding program," she said.She hoped management would become easier when feral, unmanaged honey bee colonies die out, lowering the rate of varroa reinfestation."I can see all sorts of fascinating, wonderful things happening, despite the bad news," she said."Bees are really trying hard to overcome mites on their own."
summary: The world's worst honey bee parasite is genetically mutating, making it resistant to common treatments used to save hives.
